name: Actual Wage-Profit Curves, United States, 1947-1998

book_quotes.quote: Actual wage–proﬁt curves in any given year were deﬁned in terms of the share of the wage in the production–price value of net output per worker. In order to compare these across time, it is necessary to adjust for changes in real net output per worker over time. If we think of the money value of net output per worker (y) as the product of its price index (p) and its quantity index (yr), the wage share at a given rate of proﬁt becomes σW(r)t = w(r)t / pt·yrt.

In other words, we can create real wage curves by multiplying each year's actual wage share by a productivity index. Figure 9.19 displays the real wage curves for 1947–1998 generated by the ﬁxed capital model, each plotted against its own range of proﬁt rates determined by its own maximum proﬁt rate.

Several features are striking. First, all curves have the same slightly convex shape, near-linear but not quite as much as in Krelle (1977, 306, ﬁg. 9) and Ochoa (Ochoa 1989, 424, ﬁg. 1). Second, one can distinguish the strong inﬂuence of productivity growth due to technical change, which shifts the wage-axis intercept upward in each year.
As shown in section 9 of chapter 9, we can create real wage curves by multiplying each year's actual wage share curve by a productivity index taken from the Penn World Tables PWT71, Real

Input–output tables and labor coefﬁcients for 1947–1972 were taken from Shaikh (1998a) as compiled in (Ochoa 1984). These tables were rebalanced to exclude the real estate sector, the great bulk of which is from OOH (Ochoa 1984, 252).

primary_source.agency: US Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA) + Penn World Tables (PWT 7.1) + Shaikh (1998a)/Ochoa (1984) historical compilation
primary_source.publication: BEA Use Tables and Fixed Asset Tables (for 1998 K, D, A′ matrices); historical 71-order IO tables for 1947, 1958, 1963, 1967, 1972 from Shaikh (1998a)/Ochoa (1984); Penn World Tables version 7.1 (Heston, Summers & Aten 2012, Center for International Comparisons of Production, Income and Prices, University of Pennsylvania) for the multi-year real output per worker productivity index used to deflate wage shares across years; Shaikh's appendix workbooks Appendix9_ObservedProfitRates.xlsx and Appendix9_PennWorldTables.xlsx / Appendix9_PennWorldTables2.xlsx (SalvagedInputs/book_data/ShaikhChoppedTables/)
primary_source.table_or_series_id: BEA Use Tables (1998, 65-order); Fixed Asset Tables 3.1ES/3.4ES; PWT 7.1 variables for real GDP per worker (used as productivity index yr_t / yr_0); historical IO tables 1947-1972 (71-order); appendix workbooks Appendix9_1947fixed.xlsx ... Appendix9_1998Fixed.xlsx, Appendix9_ObservedProfitRates.xlsx, Appendix9_PennWorldTables.xlsx, Appendix9_PennWorldTables2.xlsx
primary_source.url: https://www.bea.gov/industry/input-output-accounts-data ; https://www.rug.nl/ggdc/productivity/pwt/pwt-releases/pwt-7.1 (PWT 7.1 archive; CIC Penn original mirror at University of Pennsylvania)
primary_source.frequency: six benchmark cross-sections (1947, 1958, 1963, 1967, 1972, 1998), each yielding a continuous w(r) curve over r ∈ [0, R_t]
primary_source.units: wage share σW (dimensionless, fraction of net output value going to labor); real wage curve wr(r)_t = σW(r)_t · (yr_t / yr_0) in productivity units (dimensionless index relative to base-year output per worker); r in decimal profit rate; R_t in decimal maximum profit rate (Table 9.18: 1.088 for 1947 down to 0.7033 in 1972 then 0.7317 in 1998)
primary_source.license: BEA and PWT both publicly available; PWT under University of Groningen / University of Pennsylvania terms (academic free use)
primary_source.available_through: BEA iTable; PWT release archive at GGDC (https://www.rug.nl/ggdc/productivity/pwt); Shaikh's online appendix at http://www.anwarshaikhecon.org/
primary_source.replaced_by: PWT 7.1 has been superseded by PWT 10.01 (2023); for any RSCD replication post-2016 the productivity-index step should be re-anchored to the current PWT release

formula: wr(r)_t = (w(r)_t / p_t) / yr_0 = σW(r)_t · (yr_t / yr_0) (book eq. 9.23); the underlying wage share is σW(r) = 1 - r/Ra(r) = (Ra(r) - r)/Ra(r) (Appendix 9.1 eq. 9.1.10), where Ra(r) = Σ_j VR(r)_j · w_j is the convex-combination weighted average of integrated output-capital ratios (eq. 9.1.11)

components.label: Wage share curve σW(r)_t for each benchmark year t
components.role: primary object: derived from IO data + capital stock + labor coefficients for that year via the fixed-capital price system

components.label: Productivity index (yr_t / yr_0)
components.role: PWT 7.1 real GDP per worker; used to shift each year's wage share onto a common real-output axis so that curves can be compared across years

components.label: Maximum profit rate R_t (Table 9.18)
components.role: dominant eigenvalue of KT_t (one R per benchmark year); used as horizontal-axis scale (r/R) and to determine each curve's domain

components.label: BEA Use + Fixed Asset Tables (1998)
components.role: supplies A′, l′, K, D for the 1998 wage-profit curve (Fig 9.8 circ, Fig 9.12 fixed)

components.label: Ochoa (1984) / Shaikh (1998a) historical IO tables
components.role: supplies A′, l′, K, D for 1947, 1958, 1963, 1967, 1972 wage-profit curves (Fig 9.19)

concerns: PWT 7.1 (book vintage) and PWT 10.01 (current) use different base years and reference-PPP rounds; multiplicative growth-rate splice is the only safe approach
Productivity index only enters as a scalar multiplier of the wage-share curve; the underlying σW(r) for any extended benchmark year must be re-derived from a new IO + Fixed Asset extraction

extension_candidates.subseries_label: BEA Use Tables + Fixed Asset Tables 2007/2012/2017 benchmarks
extension_candidates.source: BEA Industry Economic Accounts and Fixed Asset Accounts
extension_candidates.units: current-cost industry-by-industry use ($) + current-cost net stock and depreciation ($)
concerns: Each new wage-profit curve requires full eigenvalue decomposition of the fixed-capital KT matrix for that year; this is the same construction as S902 for VR(r)_j
NAICS revisions across vintages break the 65-industry mapping used in the 1998 reference year

methodology_notes: S903 is the empirical wage-profit curve, computed for each benchmark year by solving the fixed-capital price system p(r) = w·l + p(r)·(A+D) + r·p(r)·K (Appendix 9.1 eq. 9.1.5) for the wage share at each r ∈ [0, R_t].

Each year's curve is plotted against its own r/R domain (Fig 9.19) because R_t differs across years (Table 9.18). To compare levels across years, the wage share is rescaled by the productivity index yr_t/yr_0 (eq. 9.23) - this is what makes the curves intersect rather than nest.

The wage-profit curves are 'actual' in the sense that they use the observed input-output structure (A′, K, D, l′) for each benchmark year - not a theoretical example. The curves are near-linear (slightly convex) because the underlying VR(r)_j paths are near-linear (S902 result).

The same dataset feeds Fig 9.8 (1998 circulating-capital w-p curve) and Fig 9.12 (1998 fixed-capital w-p curve); these are special-case single-year visualizations of the multi-year construction in Fig 9.19.

Table 9.19 (p. 423) shows that observed normal-capacity output-capital ratios stay within 10% of the standard ratio R across 1947-1998, justifying using the trend in the observed capacity-output ratio as a good index of technical change direction.
Productivity-index source: Appendix 9.2 Sec V explicitly names PWT 7.1 ('Real' truncated in PDF text on p.869 - likely 'Real GDP per worker, chain-weighted').
open_questions: Series count correctness (per Phase 3 Ch9 mandate): the figure list includes Fig 9.6, 9.7, 9.13-9.16, 9.18 which belong primarily to S902 (standard prices) or S901 (market-vs-direct prices). The 'core' S903 content is the wage-profit curves themselves (Fig 9.8, 9.12, 9.19). Recommend trimming the figure list; see CH9_RESEARCH_SUMMARY.md.

Verify the PWT 7.1 variable Shaikh uses (likely RGDPL2 or RGDPCH - real GDP per worker chain-weighted, but the PDF text is truncated at 'Real' on p.869). Phase 4 should pull Appendix9_PennWorldTables.xlsx to confirm the column name.

1947 R = 1.088 (Table 9.18) is greater than 1 - this is expected mathematically (R = 1/λ_max of KT) but means r/R ≤ 1 has r ≤ 1.088, i.e., profit rates higher than 100% are admissible in that year's geometry. Confirm this matches Shaikh's plotted x-axis range (0 to 1.134) for Fig 9.19.

Cross-reference Fig 9.19 with Robin/Volcker-style modern wage-profit replication; CD2's metadata flagged S049 as having PWT 7.1, NBER output/labor adjusted, and 'observed profit rates' as separate subseries which suggests a richer decomposition than the current single-series stub captures.

review_history.notes: Verbatim quotes from PDF pp.422-423 (Sec X) and Appendix 9.2 pp.868-869 (PWT 7.1 source citation). Construction = formula (eq. 9.23 and eq. 9.1.10). The CD2 stub's figure list overflows; recommend pruning in Phase 4.
